codeSnapshot.label: matrix_factorization.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import numpy as np
from numpy.linalg import solve

# ── 1. SVD-based Matrix Factorization (explicit ratings) ──
# R: user-item rating matrix (m users × n items), NaN = unrated
m, n, k = 5, 4, 2   # k = latent factors
R = np.array([
    [5, 3, np.nan, 1],
    [4, np.nan, 4, 1],
    [1, 1, np.nan, 5],
    [np.nan, np.nan, 5, 4],
    [np.nan, np.nan, 4, 4],
])

np.random.seed(42)
P = np.random.normal(0, 0.1, (m, k))   # user factor matrix
Q = np.random.normal(0, 0.1, (n, k))   # item factor matrix

# ── 2. SGD for matrix factorization (Funk SVD) ──
lr = 0.01
reg = 0.02
for epoch in range(100):
    for u in range(m):
        for i in range(n):
            if not np.isnan(R[u, i]):
                e_ui = R[u, i] - P[u] @ Q[i]
                P[u] += lr * (e_ui * Q[i] - reg * P[u])
                Q[i] += lr * (e_ui * P[u] - reg * Q[i])

R_hat = P @ Q.T
print('Predicted ratings:')
print(R_hat.round(2))

# ── 3. ALS — alternating closed-form updates ──
# Fix Q, solve for P; fix P, solve for Q
def als_step(R, fixed, reg, k):
    n_users = R.shape[0]
    result = np.zeros((n_users, k))
    for u in range(n_users):
        rated = ~np.isnan(R[u])
        Q_rated = fixed[rated]
        r_u = R[u, rated]
        # Closed form: P_u = (Q_ratedᵀQ_rated + λI)⁻¹ Q_ratedᵀ r_u
        result[u] = solve(Q_rated.T @ Q_rated + reg * np.eye(k), Q_rated.T @ r_u)
    return result

for _ in range(20):
    P = als_step(R, Q, reg=0.1, k=k)
    Q = als_step(R.T, P, reg=0.1, k=k)

print('ALS predictions:', (P @ Q.T).round(2))

# ── 4. BPR (Bayesian Personalized Ranking) — implicit feedback ──
# Maximises: P(r_{u,i} > r_{u,j}) for observed positive i, unobserved j
# Uses SGD on triplets (u, i, j): u=user, i=positive, j=negative sample
# Loss: -Σ ln σ(x_{ui} − x_{uj}) + λ‖θ‖²
# where x_{ui} = p_u · q_i  (dot product score)
# BPR is the standard for implicit feedback (purchase/click data)
# Use the 'cornac' or 'surprise' library for production BPR

eli3: Matrix Factorization decomposes the huge user-item rating matrix into two smaller matrices: a user matrix and an item matrix, each with k latent 'hidden' dimensions. User row × item column gives the predicted rating. The k dimensions capture abstract concepts — for movies, one dimension might represent 'action-ness', another 'drama-ness' — but they're learned automatically, not hand-designed.

Think of it as a compression algorithm. Instead of storing millions of (user, item, rating) triplets, you store m×k numbers for users and n×k numbers for items (much smaller). The product P·Qᵀ approximates the full rating matrix, and the latent factors capture the underlying structure that explains why users rate certain items similarly.

Two key training algorithms: SGD (Stochastic Gradient Descent — update P and Q simultaneously using one observed rating at a time) and ALS (Alternating Least Squares — fix one matrix and solve for the other in closed form, alternating). For implicit feedback (no explicit ratings, just clicks/purchases), BPR (Bayesian Personalized Ranking) is used — it learns to rank observed items above unobserved ones.

whenWhy.title: Sparse rating matrices at scale
whenWhy.body: In a system with 1 million users and 100,000 items, the full rating matrix has 10¹¹ entries — but a typical user rates only 50–200 items, leaving 99.98% unobserved. Memory-based CF must operate on this sparse matrix directly. Matrix factorization compresses the data into user and item factor matrices (1M × 50 + 100k × 50 = 55 million parameters), enabling full-matrix imputation and recommendations in O(k) per user-item pair at inference time.

whenWhy.title: Implicit feedback recommendation (no ratings available)
whenWhy.body: Most real-world interaction data is implicit: purchases, clicks, views, play duration. There are no explicit 1–5 ratings. BPR (Bayesian Personalized Ranking) and ALS with confidence weights (Hu et al., 2008) are the standard matrix factorization approaches for implicit data. They model preferences as a ranking problem — observed interactions are preferred over unobserved ones — rather than a regression problem. Spotify's music recommendation and Amazon's purchase prediction are historically built on implicit MF.

whenWhy.title: Latent factor discovery
whenWhy.body: The k latent dimensions learned by matrix factorization often correspond to interpretable concepts — genre clusters in movies, style clusters in fashion, topic clusters in documents — without being explicitly defined. This makes MF a tool for exploratory analysis as well as recommendation. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a special case of SVD with orthogonality constraints; NMF (Non-negative Matrix Factorization) adds non-negativity constraints that tend to produce more interpretable 'parts-based' decompositions.

deepDiveIntro: The rating matrix R ∈ ℝ^{m×n} is approximated as R ≈ PQᵀ, where P ∈ ℝ^{m×k} (user factors) and Q ∈ ℝ^{n×k} (item factors). The objective (regularised): min_{P,Q} Σ_{(u,i)∈Ω} (r_{u,i} − pᵤᵀqᵢ)² + λ(‖P‖²_F + ‖Q‖²_F), where Ω = set of observed (user, item) pairs. Koren, Bell & Volinsky (2009) add biases: r̂_{u,i} = μ + b_u + b_i + pᵤᵀqᵢ, where μ = global mean, b_u = user bias, b_i = item bias. Biases absorb systematic offsets (harsh raters, popular items), improving prediction accuracy. [1]

Funk SVD (Simon Funk, 2006, Netflix Prize): SGD update rules — ε_{u,i} = r_{u,i} − r̂_{u,i}; p_u ← p_u + η(ε_{u,i}·q_i − λ·p_u); q_i ← q_i + η(ε_{u,i}·p_u − λ·q_i). ALS update (closed form, parallelizable): p_u = (QᵀQ + λI)⁻¹Qᵀr_u for each user u independently. ALS is preferred for implicit feedback because all (u,i) pairs appear in the loss (including unobserved), making the matrix system dense and closed-form solvable per user. [2]

deepDive.heading: Funk SVD and the Netflix Prize
deepDive.body: Simon Funk's 2006 blog post describing a simple SGD-based SVD approach on the Netflix Prize dataset became foundational. The key insight: don't compute the full SVD of a sparse matrix (computationally expensive, requires imputation of missing values); instead, directly minimize the squared error on observed ratings via SGD. The model r̂_{u,i} = μ + b_u + b_i + pᵤᵀqᵢ, trained with biases + regularisation, achieved top-5 performance on the Netflix Prize and inspired all subsequent latent factor approaches. NMF (Lee & Seung, 1999) adds the constraint P,Q ≥ 0, producing 'additive parts' decomposition — interpretable as 'this user likes topic A + topic C,' rather than allowing negative factor cancellations. [1]
deepDive.formula: r̂_{u,i} = μ + b_u + b_i + pᵤᵀqᵢ   [with global mean and user/item biases]

deepDive.heading: BPR: Bayesian Personalized Ranking for Implicit Feedback
deepDive.body: BPR (Rendle et al., 2009) maximises the posterior P(Θ|>) where > is the observed preference relation: if user u interacted with item i but not item j, then i >_u j. Objective: max_{Θ} Σ_{(u,i,j)∈DS} ln σ(x̂_{uij}) − λ‖Θ‖², where x̂_{uij} = r̂_{u,i} − r̂_{u,j} and DS = {(u,i,j) | u interacted with i, not with j}. SGD on random triplets: sample (u, i, j), compute x̂_{uij}, gradient ∝ σ(−x̂_{uij})·∇_{Θ}x̂_{uij}. BPR directly optimises ranking quality (NDCG, AUC) rather than rating prediction RMSE — important because recommendation is fundamentally a ranking task. [2]
deepDive.formula: BPR objective: max Σ_{(u,i,j)} ln σ(x̂_{uij}) − λ‖Θ‖²,   x̂_{uij} = r̂_{u,i} − r̂_{u,j}

deepDive.heading: Evaluation: RMSE vs Ranking Metrics
deepDive.body: Matrix factorization for explicit ratings is often evaluated by RMSE = √(Σ(r_{u,i}−r̂_{u,i})²/|Ω|). This was the Netflix Prize metric — but RMSE does not directly measure recommendation quality. A model that perfectly predicts that a user will rate 3.0 vs 3.1 doesn't help ranking. Better metrics for production recommendation: NDCG@K (Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain — gives more credit for relevant items at the top of the list), Precision@K, Recall@K, Hit Rate@K. BPR-optimized models typically outperform RMSE-optimised models on NDCG@10 by 5–15%, even with higher RMSE. Choose your training objective to match your evaluation metric. [1]

youtubeLabel: Matrix Factorization for Recommender Systems — Standford CS246
takeaways: R ≈ PQᵀ: user factors P (m×k) × item factors Q (n×k). Predicted rating r̂_{u,i} = μ + b_u + b_i + pᵤᵀqᵢ. [1]
SGD (Funk SVD): update P,Q on observed ratings. ALS: alternating closed-form updates, preferred for implicit data. [2]
BPR: for implicit feedback. Optimises ranking directly via triplets (u, positive item, negative item). [2]
k (latent factors): typically 50–200. More factors → better fit, slower training, risk of overfitting. [1]
Evaluation: RMSE for explicit ratings, NDCG@K / Precision@K for recommendation quality. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between SVD, Funk SVD, and truncated SVD in this context?
edgeCases.a: True SVD: R = UΣVᵀ, exact decomposition, requires complete matrix (no missing values). Computing SVD on a sparse matrix with NaN entries requires imputation (fill with 0 or mean), then SVD — but imputed values dominate and bias the result. Truncated SVD (TruncatedSVD in sklearn): computes only the top k singular values, but still requires a complete matrix. Funk SVD: directly minimizes MSE on observed ratings only via SGD — no imputation needed, the loss only runs over Ω (observed pairs). Funk SVD is not mathematically SVD but produces a factorization R ≈ PQᵀ optimised for the observed ratings. In practice, 'matrix factorization for recommender systems' almost always means Funk-style SGD or ALS, not true SVD.

edgeCases.q: How do you choose k (number of latent factors) in practice?
edgeCases.a: k controls the capacity of the model. Too small k: underfits — can't capture complex user preferences. Too large k: overfits — memorizes training ratings, poor generalisation to unobserved entries. Typical range: k = 20–200 for most recommendation problems. Tuning: (1) Grid search over k ∈ {20, 50, 100, 200}, evaluate on a held-out validation set using NDCG@10 or RMSE. (2) Regularisation strength λ interacts with k — larger k needs stronger regularisation. Always tune (k, λ) jointly. Rule of thumb: k=50 with λ=0.01–0.1 is a strong starting point for most datasets. Large-scale systems (Spotify, Netflix) use k=100–512 and tune per A/B experiment rather than offline evaluation.

sources.label: Koren, Y., Bell, R. & Volinsky, C. — Matrix Factorization Techniques for Recommender Systems, IEEE Computer 42(8), 2009
Rendle, S. et al. — BPR: Bayesian Personalized Ranking from Implicit Feedback, UAI 2009
